title: Dyeing with goldenrod
subtitle: Extracting a clear yellow from Solidago flowers and leaves on alum-mordanted wool
excerpt: Goldenrod (Solidago canadensis and related species) is one of the most generous dye plants available to UK growers: it produces a strong, warm yellow with alum mordant that shifts to olive-gold with an iron afterbath. The flowers at peak bloom give the most saturated colour, but leaves and stems also yield usable dye.
sourceNotes: Goldenrod as a dye plant is documented in Jenny Dean, Wild Colour (1999) and Dominique Cardon, Natural Dyes (2007). Alum mordant protocol follows standard UK natural dyeing practice.

attrs.body: Wear gloves when handling alum and ferrous sulphate. Keep dye pots separate from food equipment permanently.
attrs.title: Mordant safety

content.text: Goldenrod is harvested just as the flower heads open for maximum dye yield. Use plant material at a ratio of at least 1:1 by
: 100 g of wool needs at least 100 g of fresh plant. After dyeing with an
shifts the yellow to an olive-green, useful for natural colour palettes without any synthetic dye.

items.name: Wool yarn, scoured and wet out
Fresh goldenrod flowers and upper leaves at peak bloom
Stainless steel dye pot, minimum 5 litre

content.text: Dissolve 15 g alum in a small amount of hot water. Add to the dye pot with enough water to cover the wool. Add the wet wool, heat slowly to 80°C and hold for 45 minutes. Do not boil. Remove, squeeze gently, and set aside.

Pack the goldenrod stems, leaves, and flower heads into the dye pot with enough water to cover. Heat to a simmer (80°C) and hold for 45 minutes. Strain out all plant material and return the liquid to the pot. The bath should be a strong amber-yellow.

Add the mordanted wool to the dye bath at the same temperature. Heat slowly to 80°C and hold for 45-60 minutes. Turn the wool gently every 10 minutes. Remove and rinse in descending temperature rinses.

Dissolve 2-3 g of ferrous sulphate in 5 litres of warm water. Immerse the rinsed, dyed wool for 5-10 minutes at around 50°C. The colour will shift visibly from yellow toward olive. Rinse again thoroughly and dry away from sun. Ferrous sulphate left in the fibre at high concentrations can degrade wool over time, so rinse thoroughly.

items.fix: Harvest at peak bloom when flower heads are fully open. Flowers give a cleaner yellow than leaves alone.
items.cause: Leaves were used without flowers, or the plant was harvested late in the season when tannins dominate
items.problem: The dye bath is green rather than yellow

items.fix: Use no more than 2-3 g per 5 litres and check the colour after 5 minutes. Rinse immediately once the desired shift is reached.
items.cause: Too much ferrous sulphate or left in the bath too long
items.problem: The iron afterbath produced very dark, greenish-black rather than olive
